title: vimeo_1191691601
displayTitle: 2026-05-13 - Social Services and Community Committee - Trust Horizon (Trust Variation) Bill
description: Wednesday, 13th May 2026 | Social Services and Community Committee Trust Horizon (Trust Variation) Bill Trust Horizon David Glover, Chairman Derek Caudwell, Chief Executive Jonathan Orpin-Dowell, Barrister
descriptionSummary: Trust Horizon (Trust Variation) Bill. Trust Horizon. David Glover, Chairman

chunks.text: Wait, get the recording underway, and then crack into it. Hello. All right, there we go. We've got recording is live. Oh, my God. Well, good afternoon, and welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee. Great to have you here this afternoon. Thanks. You've traveled a long way. So we've got 30 minutes on the Trusts Horizon Trust Variations Bill. We've got first up a 30-minute public hearing with David Glover, chairman, and Derek Caldwell, chief executive of Trusts Horizon.

So look, we have material from you. Oh, and Legal... got the lawyer, the leading counsel, so thank you for that point out from a great member, Danica Goedeker. So look, the time is yours. If you want to leave time for questions, please feel free, please. So look, we're in your hands. Kia ora. Tēnā, tēnā koutou katoa. I'm David Glover. I'm the chair of the trust.

This is Derek. We saw this on Zoom last time around, and I think Jonathan was here in person. So it's great to be in person this time around and answer your questions. That is a very important bill from our point of view, at least. So, the first thing I just wanted to cover off was that following the last meeting, there was a request that maybe the company might attend the next meeting, and we sent that letter on the 5th saying that the chair and the chief exec are overseas.

And hopefully, we've gone some way to answering some of the, certainly, the legal questions around the relationship between the company and the trust. But they're very happy to appear in front of the committee should you still require that at some point in the future. Thank you. And we can talk about any of those issues today if you want to pursue those. We really want to spend most of the time giving you the opportunity to ask us questions, so we don't have a long preamble. Hopefully, you had a chance to receive the

note that came through quite late yesterday, so I appreciate the impulse on your time. So what I'd invite Derek, our chief exec, to do is just at a very high level, cover those points. Right. And if you want to then dive into deeper specifics as we go through, that might be a way to structure the conversation. Sounds good. Does that work for the committee?

Yeah, excellent. No, thank you. Thank you. Over to you, Derek. Thank you. Tēnā koutou katoa. Yeah, so we structured our submission in relation to the 15 questions that we received. And we broke that down further into six sort of thematic areas.

And we thought it would be good to touch on each thematic area briefly, and then happy to sort of take questions on each area as we go through it, if that makes sense. Yep. So the six areas that we've outlined in our submission, why does the problem, an accumulation of capital, justify the solution in relation to broadening the trust purposes?

And ultimately, this comes down to trustees are actually obliged to consider broadening the purposes when we do have an accumulation of capital in the trust. Could the problem be addressed by proactively seeking projects that fund energy-related purposes? I think this was sort of a key point, and so we really wanted to outline to the committee just what we do in this space.

Over our 30 years, we've been granting over 40% into proactive projects that are energy-related, that are initiated from the trust, and we're very proud of that history. And we'll continue to do that as well. And we've put some modeling in there to sort of show even if we were to double how much we could proactively grant, we'd still have the structural issue that we're coming to you today with.

Can the problem be addressed by a non-legislative method? Yeah. Effectively, what we're asking is to bring ourselves into line with those other charitable trusts that were set up at the same time back in '92. And obviously, the legal advice is that the type of change that we're wanting to make in terms of broadening our purpose could only be done through Parliament. We're not seeking a minor change to our purpose. It is a fundamental change.
Does the bill respect the original intent of the settlor?

In terms of the settlor-At the time, it was an act of Parliament. It wasn't a private settler. And in our view, what this allows is the funds to be distributed towards the highest and best use. So we feel that actually, the original tint of the settler, it was a process that had to be gone through at the time.

And so viewing it as well within trustees and Parliament's purview. What level of distribution does the trust expect to make if its charitable purposes are broadened? So we expect to be able to achieve what other charitable trusts achieve, typically around the 3% of equity, and that would enable a further 2.8 million to go back into the community on an annual basis.

So it's very significant when you look at, we're granting 3.5 million currently into the community. How will the trust remain responsive to the community if the bill is passed? So we already have very strict accountability requirements to the community through elected members, through the Charities Commission, through our annual reporting, and obligations back to the community, and those won't change.

So I'll leave it there. So happy to sort of take questions on each of the sections, if that makes sense. Yeah, thank you. I might ask the first one. Just that structural issue you were talking about that will remain unaddressed. Can you just give us a, for those watching, a bit more of an outline of what you mean by a structural issue?

So that is with the current limitation, which over... And this isn't a recent challenge. This has been going on over 30 years in terms of the accumulation of capital continues, and it will continue to accelerate, if we're not able to grant more out into the community.

And if we remain limited by energy related purposes, which from the trust perspective, we will continue to grant into energy related purposes because they're charitable. They're charitable, they're part of our kaupapa. We've had 30 years of granting into that space. So that's going to continue. What we're asking is to be able to unlock further granting into the community for things that are not energy related.

No, thank you. Next question, Helen. Hey. So, we've had some really interesting discussion in the committee about this, and it feels like the heart of the issue is, if you were to remain with the purposes that you've got, is that an opportunity to do stuff in the energy space which will perhaps be more creative and unlock potential? Or are we actually stopping other pathways by expanding the range of things that you can do?

I was born in Cara, so I know how important just addressing basic poverty is in this area, but that's the sort of part of the dispute that is going on or the questions that are being asked. Yes, and I think I'd direct you to where we've given examples of the challenge of the limitations, paragraph 62.

So, if energy related can only go so far, and typically for a project, it's about a third of the cost. And so we can grant that third, and we do, and often that acts as a catalyst for them to go and seek further funding. But they've got to raise another two thirds of that funding.

And if that two thirds isn't forthcoming, those projects don't happen, and our funding isn't utilized. Mm-hmm. So as I say, we can continue to try and think creatively about how we execute and expand our energy related funding. But that's going to rely on others to come in and provide that two to four, so for every dollar we put in, that $2 to $4, and I guess where is that coming from?

What we're saying is that could be coming from us. Rather than our dollar, it could be $3, and then they only have to go and find $1. The project and the outcome that's being sought is still the same. So we just don't agree that limiting us to only being able to fund a third of that project is a sensible outcome.

Okay, thank you. Supplementary, Maureen. So can you explain that third? Is that a commitment in the deed? And are you talking about energy related projects? So this could be any project, so like building a marae, wharekai. Generally, they are build projects.

And the third is from our experience. So effectively, the electrical components of that build typically are about a third of the cost. So that could be the electrical supply, the fire and alarm systems- Oh, I see... et cetera. Heating, ventilation.

Thank you. If I can maybe help the committee. Obviously, the trust has, its purpose at the moment is energy related purposes, but it can't fund anything that's to do with electricity because it's charitable. So it has to be charitable independently, and then it can fund the energy related component. So, charitable under the existing law is advancement of education, relief of poverty, other things beneficial to the community. There's a religious limb for historic reasons.

So, and what I think Derek is saying, and the examples in paragraph 62 of the written submissions, a marae can come along and say, "We want to increase our resilience so that in times of emergency we can provide emergency shelter to people in the region." The trust can fund the energy related component, so if they need a new generator, if they want to put solar in, they can fund that. But if the roof needs replacing or there are other things, they can't fund them because although the project itself overall is charitable, they're limited in that charitable world to the energy related

components. So when Derek says, if the trust can green light that project, but they'll still need to find two thirds of the other funding to do the other non-energy related components. So-When they first came to me and said, "We want to do wider things," one naturally thinks of new projects. But a lot of what the trust is really talking about is saying- Mm, quite... "We want to support this project, but it can't get off the ground unless we can do other non-energy related things." So it's releasing them to fund the same sort

of charitable projects, but other components. So there's an example in the submission about a food bank. They can pay for new refrigerators that are more energy efficient or freezers, but they can't put food in the fridges or the freezers to meet the community need, because it's got to be both charitable, and then only the energy related components of the charity. If that helps clarify. Yes. Supplementary, Scott.

Yeah, so I do have a supplement and another question, Chair. But surely, we all know that buildings have a relationship to energy. The more well-insulated, the fewer leaks. The energy that we don't use is the cheapest energy we've got. So I would've thought that a marae that's leaky falls into a charitable purpose, an energy related charitable purpose. Is that not how you'd interpret it?

Certainly working in the community energy space, a healthy home is an energy efficient home. Mm. It's not the electricity, it's the home. It's the wraparound. It's the hat, the floor, the shoes, and the warm coat that's around it. That's the energy bundle that you've got to keep people healthy.

Surely it's the same for a marae. So fundamentally, what keeps any building warm is the insulation. You've got to be able to heat it. So we can fund the insulation, which we do, and we fund the heating, which we do. And the roof to keep the- Well, the roof itself doesn't actually provide that insulation to the heat escaping.

It's the insulation. Well, it stops the water falling on the insulation. Yeah. Just seems to be a bit of a- Yeah. All right. New question, Scott. Yeah. So thinking about the charitable purpose, and I take your point that you've been looking, it's not simply reactively but proactively.

Are you saying that you can only contribute one third of a cost, or was that the example of the electrical cost to a larger project? Because if, for example, you wanted to make an offer to the community, anyone with a high deprivation could have access to solar plus batteries, for example, and that would help the system.

But it's only electrical gear. Would that be something that you could proactively do, or you just can't do that? Is that... In terms... Sorry, the first element of your question? First element was, I'm a little bit unclear whether you were saying you can only fund one third of a project as a charitable trust, or were you only referring to a specific project that it has a third of the electrical- Yeah. That's related.

So you could fund 100% for a charitable purpose. As long as it's energy related. As long as it's energy related. So what's to stop you going to the most deprived in the community, and we can pick them up through the deprivation index, and saying, "We offer X amount of solar plus batteries to help reduce your electricity bills." Is there anything that is... And would you still do that?

I guess, fundamentally, as long as we could show it was relief of poverty, then- Well, lowered electricity bills... then it would come within our purview. Yeah. Lowered electricity bills would certainly help relieve poverty. And I think there's a couple of things that can be added to that.

One is in relation to the insulation space, as we explained in the submission, the trust has now insulated, over its life, 60% of the homes in the district it serves. Which I think is really pretty phenomenal when you bear in mind that not everyone would meet the deprivation index, so you couldn't do 100% anyway. You've got new homes that have been constructed which won't need it.

So it illustrates the extent of the work that the trust has done historically in that space. And there's also examples in the paper about the provision of, for example, interest-free grants to put in solar systems in community organizations. So that's certainly work that can be done as an energy related purpose. But there are limits to how widely that can go because at a certain point, you're providing private benefits, not charitable benefits, if you're going and making grants to individual people.

Which is a fundamental tension in all charities law, that you've got to choose projects that are meeting community need rather than grants to individuals. So that's a constraint that always exists there, but it's one of a purpose that could be met in addition to others if the purposes that the trust can meet are expanded.

So supplement. So I guess, ECA has the program, the Warm Kiwi Homes program. Mm-hmm. That serves a wider public good insulating homes. And you can argue that it could do more, and we believe it could do more. But the same purpose could be met, the same social purpose could be met with solar plus batteries, which would also help develop community resilience and reduce congestion on the grid.

So it would have wide system benefits as well as great public benefits in reducing poverty in our community. And yes, of course, it would go into individual homes. But in much the same way that the Warm Kiwi Homes program uses the deprivation index to have wider public benefit. Is that not something that you could consider? And to come back to the Stretch this up a little bit. To come back to the- We do need to... warmer Kiwi homes.

Warmer Kiwi homes. We know that just insulation and underfloor is not enough. We can do so much more with retrofitting wall insulation, double glazing, ventilation, removal of black mould. Those things are really critical as well. So surely there's a broader spectrum here. If the broader question is, are there more innovative energy related things we can do in the future? Absolutely. We didn't have solar 20 years ago.

We did, started off with warmer Kiwi homes very early and insulation. That's coming to a natural end or in terms of size of opportunity, and there's new technology. So of course, we will continue to look at that. But I think one of the charts in the submission says even if we doubled our proactive granting, we would still have this structural problem of our assets growing faster than our ability to grant.

So I think encouraging us to be creative in that space, absolutely. That's the space we've been operating for many years, and we will continue to innovate. It's not an either/or from our point of view, because the financial growth will still happen if we believe, if we double, say, instead of 40% of the granting being proactive, we double that, we will still have this issue of a surplus, which we would like the community to benefit from on a consistent basis. So support the idea of exploring more innovative ideas completely.

And just if I may. So, our focus has always been on best and most efficient use of our resources. So you will have seen, in our submission, we're heavily invested in community solar. So where we can support Marae, council, community halls and others to put large scale solar on, which has a wider benefit, schools as well.

So that the benefit of those facilities can be shared amongst the broader community, and that's a much more efficient approach. Excellent. I'll now move to the local Member of Parliament and the sponsor of the bill, who has a question. Or two. Thank you. I have a question because you'll remember that in the previous hearing, Dr. Victor Luca raised the issue of perhaps the trust could invest in research and development to grow new and innovative energy related projects that would potentially benefit the region. And my concern is that if you do that, then the money is

then spent on research and development and on consultants and reports and not on the front line when we already have a multitude of people who have projects and ideas. So I'm just interested in your view on, or the trust view on that approach. Yeah, absolutely. In the trust history, we have invested in research and development areas.

We've looked across the region in terms of where there are opportunities for generation back in the early 2000s, and then people could utilize, the community could utilize that in terms of actually where generation was sited, et cetera. But fundamentally, we're about enabling our capital back into the community for real tangible projects in the community.

So for any research project, it would naturally need to be limited to something that sort of benefits our district. Okay. Thank you. A supplemental? No, I'm . Oh, okay. Maureen. Look, one of the suggestions I had in my, one of my questions was around the trust's, the consideration of an amendment to the bill, which put a higher emphasis on energy related projects rather than just an open, around broadening of the scope of the trust.

Yeah, so we think that would be problematic, and I think James, John, are you . Sure. Yeah, happy to. Sorry, did you... I'm not sure you finished, the question. No, that's all right. Are you sure? I think you've got the message. Yeah. Got the message? Yeah. It was about the, yeah, amendment. The trust is open to exploring amendments that the committee is interested in, but my advice to the trust has been that we would be cautious about an amendment which sought to constitutionalize, if I can use that word, through the terms of the trust,

ratios or limits of where the money needs to go. And the reason is that we're effectively here because we've got that at the moment. We're being directed down to one path and the trust started with $5 billion, it's now got 220. So it's a tremendous community success story, and it faces a tremendously enviable problem of having too much money.

But we don't really want to have to come back to you, as pleasant as you all are, as much as we enjoy talking to you, in 15 years to make further amendments. And what we would really like to do is to put the trust on the same footing as the other charitable trusts that were established under the reforms and let the decisions about what are great ideas that members have on particular projects be assessed by the trustees who are locally elected.

And they can say, "Yes, this year some solar is the best way to spend our money that's going to give the best bang for buck into the community. But next year it might be something that doesn't have an energy link in terms of a school or a Marae or some other healthcare facility that's needed in the community." And I guess the real plea that the trustees are making to you, is that they would like those decisions to be able to be made locally about which projects to support, which is the case for all the other trusts that we're aware of

that were established under this process. And it seems really an accident of history that the good people of Eastern Bay of Plenty are left with that particular focus. So we're happy to have a discussion around that if there are particular things that members would be interested in. Just a supplementary on that. If I can just, so there's a-I'm, Central Otago's part of my region, and I'm well aware the trust there is very successful, and they have broad latitude to do things within their deed.

So I'm just curious why, yeah, why did- Yeah, why was it so narrow? Why did you end up with one different from everyone else? I think that the shortest answer to that is the people that were tasked with developing these deeds were the local electric power boards, which were being disestablished. So although it's made at a community level, it's not made by the community in the sense of people coming together and collectively saying, "What do we want to do with this asset, which is effectively going to come to us as part

of the deregulation?" And, if I was being cynical, I don't think there's any documents which show why the decision was made, but it does rather look like the purposes were kept narrow so that they were as closely aligned to what the company would be doing rather than the community. The other thing I think that one can see from looking at the document is there's a clause in the trustee that required a review after five years about whether it should continue. And it was possibly thought, I think, if you look back, that if they were only going to have about $5 million, you've got a large region

with a lot of money, that that money might be spent and there might not be much more to do, and it's become much more successful, I suspect, than anyone envisaged, and so has created this happy problem. Laura. Thank you, Mr. Chair. My question is a little bit about the company side of things back to the trust and the amount that is coming across to you.

Can we have a bit of details about the amounts, and is it from the company perspective enough for them to enable to do their business well? Because there's been some cases historically in the last five or so years of, say, Marae being cut off. I know in Galatea, for example, one of my members of Parliament's hometown, so there was a big issue there that the community was essentially cut off from electricity because of weather and then line maintenance and stuff.

So that's a huge issue for a really small, vulnerable community. And I'm just wondering whether the dividend or the amount that's coming back into your trust is actually the right amount. And getting a bit more around the reason of those kind of failures and should there actually be some of the stuff in the company that they can actually deal with some of the infrastructure side of things, I guess.

Because that key for infrastructure is really important. But yeah, I don't understand the dynamics, I guess, very well. It might be something you have to send back to the committee later and totally appreciate that. Yeah. So just- Oh, you go. Yeah. At a high level, so as a trust, the company is just one investment.

Mm-hmm. And the company itself, the lines distribution component of that company is only about 10% of its revenue. Okay. So, their directors are independent of the trust. Mm-hmm. They have fiduciary duties- Yeah... to make sure that the lines business is well maintained, it's resilient, and they have accountability back to the community as well.

Mm-hmm. As you know, when the power goes out, the community-... is very quick- Yeah... to let their feelings known. And particularly when it's a big event, whether it's Cyclone Cook, Debbie, or others, it's a very challenging time. I used to be the network manager for the company.

Mm-hmm. And it takes a concerted effort and a long period of time to get everyone back on. We've got to focus on getting sort of the bulk of customers back on as quickly as we possibly can, and a huge amount of time and effort goes into that. New question, Scott. So my question, and I'll try and be succinct, is about the experience you have, and you mentioned you're previously a network manager, Derek, but the experience you have in what is a really fast-moving energy transition we're underway to look at the opportunities,

not just Marae scale, but the distributed energy resources. Do you think you really are seeing all of the opportunities that are out there? Because from my perspective, I think there's so much you could do, so much you could do right now. Yes, I think, and sort of talked about this previously.

There's a community capability and capacity constraint as well. It's not just funding. So we have an example of community scale solar battery project in Tikaha that's been sort of in the making for about three years. We've been involved right from the outset, and they actually acknowledged that without the trust's support, it probably wouldn't have got to where it is, because they were going down the track of just a solar installation without battery, et cetera.

So we not only provide funding and backing for these projects, we actually provide a level of technical support as well for them too. So, yes, there are other areas within our communities that we'd love to see bigger scale projects and opportunities, but we've got to work with the community to make those happen.

Last question, Maureen. Actually, it- Oh, that was it. Covered? Yeah, I think Scott's covered it. My question was the same, and it's one that we've discussed, is about how are you sure that you've saturated all the opportunities? Yeah, and I think the key is we haven't, right? So we're not saying there are not more opportunities that we can direct our money towards. What we're saying is when we model it out and we make some assumptions about even if we're doubling what we're doing in that space, we will still have this problem

if we don't do something to actually broaden our purpose. Well, thank you very much for coming in this afternoon, David, Derek, and Jonathan. Thank you. Appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you very much for the committee's time. Thank you. Thanks for travel- Looking at more answers
